sentences.text: Yes.
We were only one week.
And then again we were separated from my aunt.
We were taken away, again to the showers.
Our hair was shaved again.
We were taken away, and this time the train ride wasn't very long.
I don't recall.
Just during the day, we arrived at a destination called Plaszow.
P-L-A-S-Z-O-W, which I know now is just a very short distance from Auschwitz.
And it is near the city Krakow.
We were in this camp, Plaszow, where, by the way, Schindler operated.
But I didn't know about Schindler then.
This is where he compiled his list, the famous Schindler's List.
I saw the Schindler people in Plaszow, but we didn't know that they were Schindler people.
We saw a group of people, men and women, going to work, wearing human clothes, women having their hair.
Men having sometimes caps on their head, sometimes not, but they, too, had some hair on their head.
They looked like people.
They were not skeletal.
And we never knew they were Jews.
We thought that they were possibly Polish prisoners of war, or political prisoners.
Later I found out.
There are many things that I know now, having read the book that these were Schindler's people because I know many things about them that I recall.
But we arrived in Plaszow.
In Plaszow altogether we were only seven weeks.
This one was a work camp, so we were there for the remainder of June, the month of July.
The beginning of August, we were evacuated.
We were told that Plaszow was being evacuated.
We were put on trains.
And by the way, a few of Schindler's people were crammed into our train.
And then, as it turned out, by mistake they were also taken to Auschwitz.
I have a very vivid recollection of that simply because I remember that we were put a hundred people to a train.
And a hundred was-- into a car, to a wagon-- and a hundred was horribly crowded.
And we stood all day, and then in the evening, they said 30 more.
And those 30 more people who were squeezed in there were people with hair and with clothes.
And we found out that they were Jews who had been working in a factory, in a special place in Krakow.
So I know those were Schindler's people.
And another thing, they had toothbrushes.
And each of them had a bottle of water.
And from time to time, they would brush their teeth and rinse their mouths, and spit it out.
And we had no water to drink.
We were dying.
We were dying for water.
And these people, we were hoping they would share their water with us.
But they didn't.
I understand that they knew that they cannot share it with 100 people.
It was impossible.
But for us, it was a painful realization.
And I-- when I read I was so sorry that my mother wasn't alive anymore because I wanted to tell her, Mommy, those were the Schindler people.
We didn't know who they were.
And my mother just kept remembering for years and years that they brushed their teeth and spat the water out.
We would have gladly drunk what they spat out.
That's how the recollection-- my recollection of these people, all dressed up like people, and having toothbrushes.
At that camp, we did hard manual labor.
We worked outdoors.
We were leveling the hills because we were building more and more extensions to this camp.
It was very, very hard labor.
During this seven weeks, we found out about decimation.
Decimation meant that if you did something that was against the rules, you would be lined up in the morning and every 10th person would be shot.
And at one point, we were being punished by decimation.
And we were told in the evening that in the morning we will be decimated.
And it was the most horrible night of my life because I was petrified that my mother would be shot instead of me.
Because the decimation meant that they would start counting, one, two, three, four, five, six, seven, eight, nine, and the 10th one would be shot.
And then again, one, two, three.
And I was so worried that I would be ninth, and my mother would be 10th, and I wouldn't bear it that she should be shot in front of me.
And my mother was petrified that I will be shot and she would make it.
So it was an awful, awful night, and everybody cried all night long.
And then-- this would be a long story and I cannot take up the time-- but a miracle happened.
Because during that night, there was an insurrection in a nearby factory.
Polish workers struck against the Germans.
And they were brought in and interrogated.
And all night long, they were interrogated.
And the Germans were too busy with them, and they forgot to decimate us.
And the morning came, and we stood on appel, and we were not decimated.
We went back to work.
It's a long story, but all I can tell you is that we lived through decimation.
And if I would have been probably a little older, my hair would have turned gray that night because many others, their hair turned gray during the night.
So we experienced beatings and many, many shootings, and very, very cruel things.
One recollection in Prussia was that our foreman, who refused to beat an elderly woman when Amon Goth, who was the commandant of the camp, ordered him to do.
He just stood there for a few seconds, hesitating.
She-- he-- the SS officer ordered his dog on him.
And the dog tore off his arm.
And that was in front of us.
I saw a man's arm torn off by a dog.
And I saw the bloody stump of the shoulder stick out and the arm dangling.
The most horrible things we had seen.
We had seen people shot.
We had seen people tortured.
Prussia was a horrible place, a horrible place.
Even in Auschwitz, you did not see such naked violence.
Even though the gas chambers were in Auschwitz, and the crematoria, but you did not see the immediate violence.
It was not apparent to you as it was in Prussia.
We were brought back to Auschwitz.
And the reason I mentioned earlier that it is very near-- and I'm told that about an hour's ride from Plaszow-- it took us three days-- I don't know why-- to get to Auschwitz.
But many people died on that ride because we were crowded.
There was no water to drink.
There was no air to breathe.
The weaker ones were trampled underfoot.
My mother fainted quite early and she was unconscious most of the journey.
When we arrived, the doors opened and we are again in Auschwitz.
And we were marched up, and as we were marching--they didn't take time out to select us.
But as we were marching, the selection was taking place.
Officers, SS officers in-- on motorcycles were passing us and pulling people out of the lines who were too weak to walk.
And at this point, my mother almost not made it because she was very weak, and she was just dragging herself.
And this SS officer stopped the motorcycle, reached and stuck her, hit her on the head with a stick and said, hey, old lady.
And he spoke Hungarian.
He was a Hungarian volksdeutsche who had volunteered for the SS army.
And he says, hey, grandma, can you work?
And my mother wanted to say, no because she couldn't barely walk.
And I gave her a pull, and I said, say yes.
Say yes.
Say yes.
And she said, like a bird, she was screeching, and she said, well, if I have to-- that's how her voice came out-- I will.
And so he let her go, and we marched on.
And this is when we got our numbers tattooed on our arms.
We were lined up.
And then we were driven into barracks.
And that day, an accident happened in the barracks.
We were driven-- these were proper barracks.
This was Auschwitz, not Birkenau.
These were brick barracks, much taller than the ones we had in Birkenau.
It had four-tiered beds.
On every square, 12 women had to sleep.
There was no mattress.
You slept on the wooden plank.
And you were given one blanket to cover yourself for 12.
And we were on the bottom tier.
The-- I noticed that one plank above us was cracked.
And I came out and I told the women to please move off.
And I ran off to tell the blockalteste, or the head of our barrack, who was also a Jewish girl.
But she was already very hard.
And she had been there two years.

So she-- when I wanted to talk to her to tell her somebody should replace that plank because it will break, she became very angry at me because it seems I addressed her when she was trying to talk to somebody else.
So she gave me a slap on my face.
Such as slap-- I have very high cheekbones, and by now I was pretty skinny-- that the my skin broke, split.
And I had immediately blood coming out.
And I staggered backwards.
And of course, I ran back to my barrack.
And I pleaded with the women to move off.
There is one plank which is cracked.
But everybody was hysterical because we had just come.
We just gotten our numbers.
We were exhausted.
Everybody was soaked because just before they drove us into the barrack, a sudden rainfall came.
Everybody was drenched.
And the food was being distributed.
We hadn't eaten now for-- this was the fourth day.
So everybody was hysterical trying to get the food, and they paid no attention.
So in the meantime, my mother was in a sitting position.
She had just gotten her, her food in a bowl, and she started eating.
And I was lying down because I didn't get my food yet, and I was a little dizzy.
I was lying down.
And at that point, the plank cracked, and also the one next to the broken one.
And the 12 women came crashing down, together with the two planks, on top of my mother.
So my mother was flattened like this.
Again, I must make a long story short.
My mother fractured her spine in three places.
We found that out later.
I only knew that she was not conscious.
I managed to pull her out of there.
And I put her-- you see, the barrack was divided.
It was a long brick building. 1,000 women were in each of these barracks.
And the barrack was divided by such a high structure, a brick structure, which we later found out that in the winter, it served as an oven or a stove.
It was being heated from a central place.
Now it was like a high division.
And the bunk beds were on the two sides, of course, of the barrack at the wall.
So myself and a friend, Mrs.
Greenbald-- I must mention her name.
She was [HEBREW] in Hebrew, which means she was a righteous person.
She was a most incredibly kind lady who we knew from, from Europe, from home-- not hometown, from the other town where we were taken away from.
And she and her daughter helped me carry my mother on top of this division, this oven, brick, brick structure.
And she was lying there until I managed to alert somebody.
As a matter of fact, a doctor, a young doctor who was from our home town, came and examined her and stuck needles in her foot and decided that she's not going to make it.
So she was taken away to the revere and I was told-- revere.
I'm sorry.
That's a word I should explain.
That's like a infirmary.
There was an infirmary in the place, where actually no treatment was given to anybody.
People were brought there.
If they recovered in four weeks, they could rejoin the camp.
If not, they were sent to the gas chambers.
And so my mother was taken away.
And this doctor-- Tauber was her name, young woman-- prepared me that my mother is not going to make it.
But my mother made it.
And as a matter of fact, she recovered sufficiently that when three weeks were up-- and in the meantime, this doctor from our hometown was employed in this infirmary, she got the job.
In the next few days they recruited her as a doctor there.
So she came to tell me that every four weeks the sick are selected out of the infirmary.
I should make sure to smuggle my mother out of there.
So with the help of Mrs.
Greenbald and her daughter-- who was, by the way, almost my age.
She was 14-- and another friend, who was our rabbi's daughter.
She still-- she lives here in Brooklyn.
I should mention her name.
Her name was Yolan-- or [?
Yetu, ?] we called her as a nickname-- Singer.
She was a marvelous, marvelous friend, as well.
We carried my mother out.
We snuck her out late at night.
And then we carried her back to the barracks.
And, and so the next day, or a day later when the selection took place, my mother wasn't there.
In the meantime, we were in Auschwitz, in this place, in this particular camp also for about four weeks.
The beginning of September, 500 women were to be taken away from our barrack to Germany to work in an airplane factory.
So we were going to have a selection again.
And I was very worried that my mother will not make the selection.
And the selection was done this way.
And I'm going to explain it quickly.
Everybody had to drop their dress, that gray thing.
And drop it at the end of the barrack.
And we had to line up on one side of this brick structure.
And at the opening of the barrack-- by the way, the barracks were like a-- had like a barn-- such an opening-- wide open gates, wooden gates, open.
And the selection committee stood at this end.
Mengele didn't do this particular selection-- another doctor and SS men.
And we had to line up, naked, as we proceeded to the front.
And they looked us over.
If we were not extremely skeletal, and there was no blemish or anything on our bodies, then they sent us out right away.
It was raining, but they took-- sent us out.
And the others who did not make it were sent to go around the other side of the brick division.
So as we were going, I was holding my mother.
By the way, now months passed.
In the meantime, my mother recovered sufficiently to walk.
She was very clumsy with her hands, and she remained-- especially several fingers remained completely immobile.
And also she remained slightly in a bent position from this injury.
And she dragged one leg, but she was able to walk and unaided.
But she couldn't do things quickly.
She needed help with everything.
Even eating, she needed help.
She was an invalid.
But her body was without blemish, and she wasn't extremely skeletal yet.
So-- and neither was I.
We were probably not heavy people before the war.
We were both thin people.
And amazingly, thin people remained in better physical condition longer than fat people.
Fat people lost their fat quickly, and became skeletal very quickly.
And most of them died also faster.
It seems that you were used to eating less before the war.
So I imagine that the very small rations in the camp somehow were more bearable for people who hadn't eaten too much before.
I imagine.
Some study should be done on this.
So we came to the selection, and they looked at my mother and she was driven out.
They looked at me.
On my right foot-- and you could see this scar here-- I had had an injury.
Somebody kicked me in one of the train rides.
And it started festering.
And by now, it was gangrenous.
It was very smelly.
My leg was swollen.
And the hole so deep that you could see the bone.
You could look in there, and there was the bone.
And it was oozing this horrible yellow liquid.
So the leg was looked bad, but the rest of my body was fine.
So they looked me over, and they were going to send me out, but one of them pointed.
So the selection officer looked there and said, ah.
She's not going to last too long with this injury.
So they sent me to the other side of the partition, and I was separated from my mother.
So when the selection was over, there was a handful of us who were-- remained naked.
We were going to be sent to the gas chambers.
I didn't care where I'm being sent.
I only wanted to get to my mother because I knew that without me, she'll not make it.
They will discover very soon that she is an invalid, and she's going to be killed.
Maybe somebody will shoot her or somebody will beat her to death because she can't move fast.
So I persuaded one of the young girls who was crying because her two sisters were selected out to come with me.
We should jump over this and join because the line was still going.
But she was afraid.
She said no, they'll shoot us.
They'll notice it.
Anyway, this is a long story in itself, but they gave us our dresses back.
And we were going to be taken out on the other-- the other entrance to the gas chambers.
But suddenly, I couldn't wait any longer because I saw that the line is already finishing.
The naked bodies are lining up and soon it's over.
My chance, this is my chance.
So I quickly took my dress off.
I was naked like the others.
Nobody noticed me.
I hopped over.
And luckily, I was always very, very athletic.
I did all kinds of sports.
It was very high, and it wasn't easy to do this.
I quickly hopped over that partition and joined the end of that line.
But the dress that I took off did not drop.
I was holding it, draping it against my leg.
And it was a miracle.
The SS people were in a hurry by now.
It was the end.
It was raining.
They probably had to finish their job quickly.
So they said, go, go quickly.
And I was sent out to join the group.
I did not meet my mother all day.
I didn't know where she was.
I was put in a transport.
Then were taken to the showers in the evening.
And after the showers, I met my mother because my mother had a lot of trouble getting dressed.
And an SS woman was twisting her arm.
She couldn't put her dress on fast enough, so this tall, beefy SS woman was twisting her arm, and she was shrieking.
And I noticed that.
And when I noticed that, I see it's my mother.
She is going to break the arm of my mother.
So I jumped on her.
I jump on her.
You know what that means to jump on an SS woman.
I jumped on her and screamed.
I said, you are going to break her arm.
So she started to kick me and beat me with her whip.
And I fell down.
And for a short while, I lost consciousness.
And she didn't kill me.
She just beat me very badly.
I was very badly bruised, but she didn't kill me.
And so everybody was already out of the showers when my mother managed to bring me back, and called, Elli, wake up, wake up.
And then I came to.
And we went on the appel, and we were taken onto the trains.
And we survived Auschwitz, and we were away from Auschwitz.
We were taken to Germany westward to a place which is in Bavaria called Augsburg.
It's a factory town.
We were put you in a factory compound, not a concentration camp.
As a matter of fact, the SS people who were awaiting us at the train were very surprised to see who came.
They said to our leader-- there was one woman who was our spokeswoman-- they said we asked for 500 women, not for men.
And we said, but we are women.
Because we were shaven again, freshly shaven, and we were by now stick-like, so we didn't have any shape, and we looked like men.
And then, then they told us, where is your luggage?
And everybody started laughing.
Luggage?
We have no luggage.
He says, but you have no personal belongings?
And we said, no.
We have nothing.
And so it was obvious that we are now dealing with a totally new leadership here.
They don't know anything about concentration camp inmates and how to treat them.
They were very nice to us.
They were very nice.
As a matter of fact, my mother could not walk fast, so one of the SS women said, what is your name, to my mother.
So my mother looked and said my number is A17361.
And she said, I didn't ask for your number.
I asked for your name.
So she said, my name is Laura Friedmann.
So she called her Mrs.
Friedmann.
This was like, we are on another planet here.
In any case, were were taken to this just rooms we were given in the factory.
And that was our camp.
The-- soon some SS leaders joined this group.
As a matter of fact, this group weren't even SS.
They were the Wehrmacht, which is the official German army.
But they were joined by SS people in a few weeks.
And very soon, our treatment changed.
Our food changed.
By the way, in the beginning, we got food.
We got soup.
We got dumplings.
It was like human food.
But very soon that too changed, and we were given this mush, this awful mush that in all the camps we were fed once a day.
And then coffee, which wasn't coffee.
Some black liquid in the morning with a piece of bread, and then the soup, the mush, in the evening.
We were working in a factory manufacturing airplanes, bombers.
The part of the plane that I was manufacturing, by the way, was a very sensitive and a very, supposedly, highly intelligent work.

I had to put together, on a machine, you know, solder together pieces that, when it was finished, it had a small equipment, a small part of an equipment, which measured the distance of the bomb and the direction of the bomb on the plane.
It was built into the mechanism of-- the bombing mechanism.
And so this was the work we did, 12-hour shifts, one week by day, and one week by night.
It was very, very hard labor to work for 12 hours with minimum food.
But it wasn't Auschwitz, and it wasn't Plaszow.
And luckily, we were there all winter long till the end of March 1945, which was a very fortunate thing because only two people were lost to us.
One girl who, for some reason, attempted to escape and she was taken to the Dachau to be executed.
And another woman who discovered in Auschwitz that she's pregnant.
And she was taken away to the Dachau when she was already advanced in her pregnancy.
So we knew that we lost these two.
So instead of 500, we were 498 women when we were evacuated into Dachau at the end of March 1945.
Can you just tell us how--
